metadata.description: Length-matched Craigslist-style contrastive pairs with reduced dimensions (merged from 15 to 8). All pairs closely mirror the format, style, and context of actual Craigslist buyer/seller negotiations used in run_eval and lightweight_gridsearch.
metadata.usage: For each dimension, extract residual-stream activations on positive and negative examples, compute the mean difference vector, and add the scaled vector during inference to steer behavior toward that dimension.

label_convention.positive: Response demonstrating the target negotiation trait at high quality
label_convention.negative: Response lacking the trait or demonstrating the opposite behavior

metadata.notes: All contexts and responses use Craigslist buyer/seller negotiation format matching run_eval: casual 1-2 sentence responses, specific items with prices, references to item condition/comparables/pickup logistics.

dimensions.name: Firmness
dimensions.description: Standing your ground under pressure, making direct confident statements, and communicating in clear unambiguous terms without aggression or excessive hedging

pairs.context: You're selling a used iPhone 13 listed at $450 on Craigslist. The buyer messages: 'Would you take $250? I've seen cheaper ones online.'
pairs.positive: I appreciate the interest, but $450 is already competitive for an iPhone 13 in this condition. The lowest I'd go is $420.
pairs.negative: Oh, $250... I mean, maybe I could come down quite a bit. What number were you actually thinking?

pairs.context: You listed a dining table for $300 on Craigslist. A buyer says: 'That's way overpriced for a used table. I'll give you $120.'
pairs.positive: This is solid oak and barely used — $300 is fair for what it is. I'm firm at $275 minimum.
pairs.negative: Hmm, you might be right that it's a bit much. How about $150? I just want it gone honestly.

pairs.context: You're buying a used mountain bike listed at $800 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I can't go below $750, that's my bottom line.'
pairs.positive: I understand, but based on the wear I see in the photos, $650 is where I need to be. That's fair for this bike.
pairs.negative: Oh ok, I don't want to push too hard... I guess $750 could work, or maybe somewhere around there?

pairs.context: You listed a PS5 for $350 on Craigslist. A buyer says: 'I can only do $200. That's all I have.'
pairs.positive: $350 is already below retail and this console is barely used. I need at least $325 to make this work.
pairs.negative: Well... I really need to sell it, so maybe $200 isn't too far off? What if we said like $210?

pairs.context: You're selling a couch listed at $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: '$500 is steep for a used couch. Can you do $300?'
pairs.positive: This couch cost $1,800 new and it's in great shape. $500 is already a deal — I'd do $475 but that's it.
pairs.negative: I mean, I guess $300 isn't terrible... I could probably work with something around that range?

pairs.context: You're buying a used laptop listed at $600 on Craigslist. You think it's overpriced for the specs.
pairs.positive: Based on the specs and age, $400 is fair. There are newer models going for similar prices right now.
pairs.negative: I was thinking maybe around $400? But I'm not sure, what do you think would be a reasonable price?

pairs.context: You listed an acoustic guitar for $275 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'll give you $100 cash right now.'
pairs.positive: No thanks, $100 doesn't work. This guitar is worth $275 and I'm in no rush to sell it.
pairs.negative: Hmm, $100 is low but cash is nice... maybe we could figure something out? I'm pretty flexible.

pairs.context: You're selling a dresser for $200 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'My budget is really tight, could you do $80?'
pairs.positive: I understand budget constraints, but this is solid wood in excellent condition. $180 is the lowest I'll go.
pairs.negative: Oh, well... I don't want you to have to stretch. Maybe I could come down to like $100 or so?

pairs.context: You're buying a used camera listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller seems eager to sell quickly.
pairs.positive: I've checked the market and $350 is a fair offer for this model with that shutter count. That's my number.
pairs.negative: I was thinking maybe $350? But honestly I could go higher if that's too low for you.

pairs.context: You listed a washing machine for $400 on Craigslist. A buyer says: 'These go for $250 on Facebook Marketplace.'
pairs.positive: This model retails for $900 and runs perfectly. $400 is fair — I'm not matching lowball marketplace prices.
pairs.negative: Oh really? I didn't check there. Maybe I should lower it... would $260 work for you?

pairs.context: You're selling a road bike for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer keeps pushing: 'Come on, $200 is fair.'
pairs.positive: I've already come down from my listing price. $320 is my final offer and it's a good deal for this bike.
pairs.negative: I don't know, maybe you're right... I could possibly do $220 or somewhere around there if that helps?

pairs.context: You're buying a standing desk listed at $150 on Craigslist. The seller seems firm on price.
pairs.positive: I can offer $100 for the desk. It has some scratches and I'd need to rent a truck to pick it up.
pairs.negative: Would you maybe consider $100? I totally understand if not, it was just a thought I had.

dimensions.name: Empathy
dimensions.description: Recognizing and acknowledging the other party's feelings, constraints, and perspective while building trust, connection, and a positive working relationship

pairs.context: You're selling a baby crib for $200 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I just had a baby and money is really tight right now. Would you take $100?'
pairs.positive: Congratulations on the new baby! I know how expensive everything gets with a newborn. Let me see what I can do on the price.
pairs.negative: That's not my concern. The crib is $200 and that's what it costs — take it or leave it.

pairs.context: You're buying a used car listed at $8,000 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they're selling because they lost their job.
pairs.positive: I'm sorry to hear about your situation — that's really tough. I want to make sure we find a price that works for both of us.
pairs.negative: Ok, so about the car — what's the absolute lowest you'll go? I want to get a good deal on this.

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I need this for school but I'm a broke college student.'
pairs.positive: I remember those days! College budgets are rough. Let's see if we can work something out that makes sense for you.
pairs.negative: Everyone says they're broke. The laptop is $400 — that's already a good price for what you're getting.

pairs.context: You listed a set of power tools for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'These would be for my dad — he just retired and wants to get into woodworking.'
pairs.positive: That's such a nice gift idea — I'm sure he'll love having a proper set to start with. What price range works for you?
pairs.negative: Ok. I'm asking $300 for the set. Are you interested at that price or not?

pairs.context: You're buying a furniture set for $600 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they're moving across the country next week.
pairs.positive: Moving cross-country is stressful — I've been there. I hope the move goes smoothly. Let's figure out a fair price for us both.
pairs.negative: Sounds like you're in a rush then. I'll offer $300 since you clearly need this gone by next week.

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $250 on Craigslist. The buyer says they recently got a bad deal on another TV that broke after a week.
pairs.positive: That's frustrating — nothing worse than spending money on something that doesn't last. I can show you this one working before you commit.
pairs.negative: Not my problem. This TV works fine. Are you buying it at $250 or not?

pairs.context: You're buying a bike listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller mentions it was their kid's bike and they recently moved out for college.
pairs.positive: That must be bittersweet! I'm sure they had great times on this bike. I'd take good care of it if we can work out a price.
pairs.negative: Ok, so since they don't need it anymore, would you take $300? It's just sitting there collecting dust.

pairs.context: You listed a camera for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'm trying to start a photography side business but funds are limited.'
pairs.positive: That's exciting — everyone starts somewhere and this camera is great for getting started. Let's see what we can figure out.
pairs.negative: Good luck with that. The price is $350 and that's what the camera is worth.

pairs.context: You're selling a winter coat for $80 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I just moved here from Florida and wasn't prepared for how cold it gets.'
pairs.positive: Ha, the first winter up here is always a shock! This coat will definitely keep you warm — it's rated for well below zero.
pairs.negative: Ok. The coat is $80. Do you want it or not?

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $175 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they're downsizing after a difficult divorce.
pairs.positive: I'm sorry you're going through that — transitions like that are never easy. Let's work out a price that feels right for both of us.
pairs.negative: Ok. Would you take $100? Sounds like you need to clear out your stuff quickly anyway.

pairs.context: You're selling a stroller for $150 on Craigslist. The buyer mentions they're expecting twins next month.
pairs.positive: Twins! That's amazing, congratulations! This stroller has been great for us and I hope it serves your family well too.
pairs.negative: It's $150 for the stroller. Let me know if you're interested in buying it.

pairs.context: You listed gym equipment for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'm recovering from knee surgery and my doctor says I need to exercise at home.'
pairs.positive: I hope your recovery goes well — having equipment at home really does make staying consistent so much easier. This set is perfect for that.
pairs.negative: Ok. The equipment is $400. It's all in good working condition if you want it.

dimensions.name: Active Listening
dimensions.description: Demonstrating genuine attention through reflecting, summarizing, and asking probing questions to understand the other party's constraints, priorities, and decision-making criteria

pairs.context: You're selling a used MacBook for $700 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I need something reliable for work, but I've had bad experiences with used electronics and my budget is capped at $600.'
pairs.positive: So reliability is your main concern and you're working with a $600 budget — is that right? What kind of work will you be using it for?
pairs.negative: I can do $650. The MacBook works great and I've never had any issues with it personally.

pairs.context: You listed a car for $12,000 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I like the car but the mileage worries me, and I'd also need to factor in new tires.'
pairs.positive: So you're looking at two things — the mileage and the cost of new tires on top. What mileage range would make you comfortable for this model?
pairs.negative: The mileage is totally fine for this year model. I can do $11,500 — let me know if that works for you.

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $450 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I paid $2,000 for this two years ago and it's barely been used, mostly just sat in our guest room.'
pairs.positive: So it's been in a guest room and hasn't seen daily wear — that's good to know. How often did guests actually use it though?
pairs.negative: That's nice but I can only do $300. Used furniture loses value fast regardless of how much you originally paid.

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'm comparing a few bikes. Yours seems nice but the other one has newer components at the same price.'
pairs.positive: Which components on the other bike are newer? That would help me explain what makes mine worth it compared to that one.
pairs.negative: This bike is in great shape and $300 is fair. I'd honestly recommend going with mine over whatever else you're looking at.

pairs.context: You listed a gaming PC for $900 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'My main concern is whether the GPU can handle newer games, and I'm also worried about the power supply age.'
pairs.positive: So GPU performance and PSU reliability are your two big concerns — what games are you mainly looking to run? I can give you specifics on both.
pairs.negative: The GPU handles everything I've thrown at it and the PSU is fine. $900 is a good deal for these specs.

pairs.context: You're buying a refrigerator listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'It's only 3 years old, works perfectly, we're just upgrading to a bigger one for our growing family.'
pairs.positive: Got it, so it's been running well for 3 years with no issues at all? Is there anything I should know about maintenance or any quirks with it?
pairs.negative: Ok, how about $350? Three-year-old fridges lose a lot of value even if they still work.

pairs.context: You're selling a guitar for $275 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'm a beginner and not sure if this model is right for me. I don't know if the action is too high.'
pairs.positive: So you're just starting out and playability is your main worry — the action height especially. What kind of music are you looking to play?
pairs.negative: It's a great guitar for any level. $275 is my price and the action is perfectly fine on it.

pairs.context: You listed a washer/dryer set for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I need to know if these fit — my space is only 27 inches wide — and whether they stack.'
pairs.positive: 27 inches wide and you need them to stack — let me check the exact dimensions for you. Is height limited too or just the width?
pairs.negative: They're standard size and I think they stack. Want to come take a look? I'm asking $600 for the pair.

pairs.context: You're buying a standing desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'It's motorized with a lift. I bought it during COVID but I'm going back to the office now.'
pairs.positive: A motorized standing desk is nice. How has the motor been holding up? Any issues with the height adjustment over the time you've owned it?
pairs.negative: Would you take $120? Standing desks from the COVID era are everywhere now and prices have dropped.

pairs.context: You're selling patio furniture for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I like the set but I'm not sure the table fits my deck, and I'd need to figure out delivery.'
pairs.positive: So table size and delivery are the two things to solve. What are your deck dimensions? And where are you located — maybe we can figure something out.
pairs.negative: The table is a standard size and you'd need to arrange your own pickup. $350 is the price for the set.

pairs.context: You listed a kayak for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I've never owned one before. I'm worried about storage and whether it's stable enough for a beginner.'
pairs.positive: Storage and stability — both solid concerns for a first kayak. Where would you be keeping it? This model is actually one of the more stable ones out there.
pairs.negative: It's a great kayak and super stable. $400 is my price — let me know if you want it.

pairs.context: You're buying a TV listed at $300 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'It's a 2022 model, no burn-in. I'm upgrading because I want something bigger for the new living room.'
pairs.positive: No burn-in is good to hear — any dead pixels or screen issues at all? How does the picture look in a room with a lot of natural light?
pairs.negative: How about $200? Two-year-old TVs depreciate fast even if they're still in decent shape.

dimensions.name: Composure
dimensions.description: Maintaining professionalism under pressure, taking time before responding, resisting urgency to rush, and not letting emotions drive decisions

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'That's a ripoff, you could get a new one for that price. Stop trying to scam people.'
pairs.positive: I can see why you might feel that way. This model actually retails for $1,200 new — happy to share some specs that explain the price.
pairs.negative: A scam? Are you kidding me? Don't waste my time if you can't afford it.

pairs.context: You listed a couch for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'll give you $150 right now, final offer. Take it or I'm gone.'
pairs.positive: I appreciate the offer but $150 doesn't work for me. I'm not in a rush, so let me know if your budget changes.
pairs.negative: Fine, $150 it is. I just want this couch out of my house already and I'm sick of dealing with buyers.

pairs.context: You're buying a car for $10,000 on Craigslist. The seller just revealed a big scratch they didn't mention in the listing.
pairs.positive: I wish that had been in the listing, but let's not derail this. How does the scratch factor into what you'd be willing to do on price?
pairs.negative: Are you serious? You hid that from me on purpose! This is a complete waste of my time.

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer has changed their offer three times in the last hour.
pairs.positive: Let's pin down a number that works for both of us. Where exactly do you need to be to move forward on this today?
pairs.negative: Make up your mind already! I've given you three different prices and you keep changing what you want from me.

pairs.context: You listed a bike for $350 on Craigslist. A buyer says: 'I could get this same bike new on sale for $300, you're dreaming.'
pairs.positive: Fair point if there's a sale happening. This one has some upgrades though — want me to walk you through what's been added to it?
pairs.negative: Then go buy the new one! Why are you even messaging me if you think this is such a bad deal?

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller just told you two other people made offers.
pairs.positive: Good to know there's other interest. My offer is $175 — take your time deciding and let me know what you think.
pairs.negative: Wait, others are interested? Ok fine, I'll pay the full $200 right now. I don't want to lose it to someone else.

pairs.context: You're selling a PS5 for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer no-showed for pickup yesterday and just messaged asking if you still have it.
pairs.positive: I do still have it. If you'd like to reschedule, I just need a firm time and day that works for you.
pairs.negative: You already wasted my whole afternoon once. Why should I even trust you to actually show up this time?

pairs.context: You listed a guitar for $275 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'My friend says this guitar is worth $100 max.'
pairs.positive: Your friend might be thinking of a different model. I'm basing my price on recent sold listings for this exact guitar and brand.
pairs.negative: Your friend doesn't know what they're talking about. This guitar is worth exactly what I'm asking, end of story.

pairs.context: You're buying a washer listed at $300 on Craigslist. The seller suddenly raised the price to $350 because someone else offered more.
pairs.positive: I understand you got a better offer, but I'd prefer to stick with the $300 we originally discussed. Let me know if that still works.
pairs.negative: That's so shady! You already told me $300 and we had a deal. I can't believe you'd just raise the price like that.

pairs.context: You're selling furniture for $500 on Craigslist. The same buyer has sent you lowball offers every few hours all weekend.
pairs.positive: I've seen your messages. My price is $500 and I'm happy to discuss once your budget gets closer to that range.
pairs.negative: Stop spamming me with these ridiculous offers! I already told you what the price is a dozen times.

pairs.context: You listed a camera for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says your listing photos are bad and accuses you of hiding defects.
pairs.positive: I can take better photos or do a video call so you can see the full condition. Happy to be totally transparent about it.
pairs.negative: I'm not hiding anything! If you don't trust me then don't buy it. The photos show exactly what it looks like.

pairs.context: You're buying a table listed at $250 on Craigslist. The seller has taken 3 days to respond to each of your messages.
pairs.positive: No worries on the response time — I'm still interested in the table. Does $200 work for you?
pairs.negative: Finally you respond! I've been waiting forever. At this point you should be giving me a discount for wasting my time.

dimensions.name: Strategic Concession-Making
dimensions.description: Making concessions conditionally, reciprocally, and with decreasing magnitude; never giving something for nothing

pairs.context: You're selling a used MacBook for $700 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Can you come down on the price at all?'
pairs.positive: I could do $650 if you can pick it up today and pay cash. Otherwise I'm staying at $700 for the listing.
pairs.negative: Sure, how about $600? I really just want to sell it. Let me know if that works for you.

pairs.context: You listed a couch for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer offered $350, you countered at $475. They say: 'Can you go any lower?'
pairs.positive: I already came down from $500 to $475. I'd need you to come up from $350 before I can move any further.
pairs.negative: Ok, how about $425 then? I really want to get this sold before the weekend is over.

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $8,000 on Craigslist. The seller won't budge on the asking price.
pairs.positive: If you can include the winter tires in the trunk, I'll pay your full asking price of $8,000. That way we both get something.
pairs.negative: Ok, I'll just pay the $8,000 then. I don't want to lose the car by haggling over a few hundred dollars.

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer asks: 'Would you throw in the helmet and lock for free?'
pairs.positive: I'll include the helmet if you pay the full $350 for the bike. The lock I'd want $20 extra for — it's a Kryptonite.
pairs.negative: Sure, I'll throw them both in for free. I just want to get the deal done and clear out my garage.

pairs.context: You listed a TV for $300 on Craigslist. You already dropped to $275. The buyer says: 'How about $250 and we call it a deal?'
pairs.positive: I can do $260 but that's my final number. I've already come down $40 from where I started on this listing.
pairs.negative: Alright, $250 it is. I've been trying to sell this for a while now and I just want it gone honestly.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller seems like they won't budge on price.
pairs.positive: I'll offer $175 if I can come get it this evening. Saves you another weekend of dealing with buyer messages.
pairs.negative: How about $150? I know that's a pretty big drop but I figured it can't hurt to ask.

pairs.context: You're selling a washer/dryer set for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer wants to negotiate the price down.
pairs.positive: I'll come down to $550 if you can handle pickup yourself with your own truck. I can't do delivery at this price.
pairs.negative: Ok fine, $500? I just need the space cleared out honestly and I'm getting flexible on it.

pairs.context: You listed a guitar for $275 on Craigslist. The buyer offered $200 and you countered at $260. They haven't moved.
pairs.positive: I've already moved from $275 to $260 — that's as far as I go unless you come up closer to my number.
pairs.negative: You know what, let's just do $225 and be done with it. I don't want to keep going back and forth.

pairs.context: You're buying a camera listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller has come down to $375 so far.
pairs.positive: I appreciate you coming down. If you include the carrying case with it, I'll meet you right at $375.
pairs.negative: Could you maybe do $350 instead? $375 is still a little bit high for what I had in mind.

pairs.context: You're selling patio furniture for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer keeps trying to get the price lower.
pairs.positive: I'll do $325 but only if we close this today. After that I'm relisting the set back at $350 tomorrow.
pairs.negative: How about $280? I just want to clear out the patio before the weather turns this winter.

pairs.context: You listed a gaming console for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Can you at least include a game with it?'
pairs.positive: I'll include one game if you pay the full $300. If you want a lower price, the games stay as separate items.
pairs.negative: Sure, go ahead and pick whichever games you want. I'll throw in a couple at no extra charge.

pairs.context: You're buying a refrigerator listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller has come down from $500 to $475.
pairs.positive: If you can help me load it into my truck when I pick up, I'll pay the $475 right now. Deal?
pairs.negative: Could you go a bit lower still? Maybe $425? That would really work a lot better for my budget.

dimensions.name: Anchoring
dimensions.description: Setting advantageous reference points early in a negotiation; establishing the range in which discussion takes place

pairs.context: You're buying a used Honda Civic listed at $9,000 on Craigslist. You want to set a favorable price range before negotiations start.
pairs.positive: Based on the mileage and year, comparable Civics in this area are selling for $6,500 to $7,500. I'd offer $7,000.
pairs.negative: What's the lowest you'd take for it? I'm flexible on price and just want to see where you're at.

pairs.context: You're selling a MacBook Pro listed at $800 on Craigslist. The buyer asks: 'What can you do on the price?'
pairs.positive: This model sells refurbished for $1,100 from Apple. At $800 you're already saving over 25% — that is the deal.
pairs.negative: I'm open to offers honestly. What were you thinking? I don't want to price myself out of a sale.

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $600 on Craigslist. You want to establish a lower range early in the conversation.
pairs.positive: I've been watching similar couches go for $300-$400 on Marketplace this week. I'd start my offer at $350.
pairs.negative: What kind of price did you have in mind? I don't want to lowball you or offend you with my number.

pairs.context: You're selling a used PS5 for $350 on Craigslist. A buyer asks about the price before making their offer.
pairs.positive: New PS5s go for $500 and mine comes with two controllers plus five games. At $350 this is already below market.
pairs.negative: I listed it at $350 but I'm really flexible on it. What's your budget looking like?

pairs.context: You're buying a dining table listed at $400 on Craigslist. You want to anchor your offer low with data.
pairs.positive: Tables like this in similar condition are going for $200-$250 based on recent sold listings I've checked. I'd offer $225.
pairs.negative: I'm interested but $400 seems a bit high. Would you go lower? What do you think is actually fair?

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $500 on Craigslist. A buyer asks why you priced it that way.
pairs.positive: This exact model goes for $700-$800 used on eBay. Plus I just put $150 into new tires and a full tune-up. $500 is competitive.
pairs.negative: I kind of just picked a number that seemed right. I'm happy to negotiate if you think it should be lower.

pairs.context: You're buying a used washer listed at $350 on Craigslist. You want to start the negotiation on your terms.
pairs.positive: Five-year-old washers like this one typically go for $150-$200 around here. I'll offer $175 — that's fair for the age.
pairs.negative: Would you be willing to take less? I'm not sure what these actually go for but $350 feels like a lot.

pairs.context: You're selling a set of power tools for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer asks if the price is negotiable.
pairs.positive: Buying these individually new would run over $900. My price of $400 is less than half and they're barely used.
pairs.negative: I'm asking $400 but definitely let me know if that's too much. I'm sure we can figure out a number that works.

pairs.context: You're buying a used TV listed at $300 on Craigslist. You want to anchor with comparable prices.
pairs.positive: This model is two years old and I'm seeing them sell for $200-$250 used. I'll offer $220 based on those comparables.
pairs.negative: Is $300 your firm price or is there room? Just trying to get a sense of where you're at with it.

pairs.context: You're selling a leather jacket for $150 on Craigslist. A buyer messages showing interest.
pairs.positive: This jacket retails for $400 new and mine is in near-mint condition. $150 is a steal at less than half the original price.
pairs.negative: I'm asking $150 but feel free to make me an offer. I'm open to whatever you think is reasonable for it.

pairs.context: You're buying a home gym setup listed at $600 on Craigslist. You want to anchor with market data.
pairs.positive: I've been tracking used gym equipment prices and similar setups are selling for $350-$450. I'll offer $400 for the lot.
pairs.negative: What's your bottom line on this? I want to make sure we're in the same ballpark before I throw out a number.

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer asks if you'd take less for it.
pairs.positive: This camera body alone sells for $500+ used on eBay. With the lens included at $450, it's honestly a great deal.
pairs.negative: Yeah, I could probably go lower on it. What were you thinking? I'm pretty open to offers.

dimensions.name: BATNA Awareness
dimensions.description: Understanding your best alternative to a negotiated agreement; being willing to walk away; not being desperate to close

pairs.context: You're selling a couch for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'll give you $250 — take it or leave it.'
pairs.positive: I'll leave it then. I've got two other people coming to look at it this weekend and I'm in no rush to sell.
pairs.negative: Hmm, I don't have any other offers right now... ok, $250 works I guess. Let's just do it.

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $10,000 on Craigslist. The seller won't budge below $9,500.
pairs.positive: I've got another car I'm looking at for $8,500 that's comparable to yours. I'd prefer yours, but not at $9,500.
pairs.negative: I really want this specific car and I haven't found anything else I like... ok fine, $9,500 then.

pairs.context: You listed a laptop for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer threatens to go buy from someone else instead.
pairs.positive: No hard feelings — I hope you find what you're looking for. My price reflects the value and I'm not worried about selling.
pairs.negative: Wait, don't go! Let me knock it down to $450 — I'd rather sell to you than have to start all over.

pairs.context: You're buying a bike listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller pressures you to decide right now today.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the urgency, but I've got two other bikes to check out this week first. I'll decide after seeing them all.
pairs.negative: Ok, I'll take it right now — I don't want to miss out on this. $400 is fine, when can I pick it up?

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I can get a brand new one for just $350 on sale.'
pairs.positive: Then that might be the better deal for you. This TV is priced at $300 and I think that's fair for what it is.
pairs.negative: Oh no, please don't do that! What if I dropped it to $200? I really need to get this sold soon.

pairs.context: You're buying furniture on Craigslist and the seller says there are multiple other interested buyers already.
pairs.positive: That's fine — I've got a few other listings I'm looking at too. My offer is $300 and you can let me know by tomorrow.
pairs.negative: Oh really? Ok, I'll pay full price then right now. I don't want to lose this to someone else.

pairs.context: You listed a gaming console for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Nobody is going to pay that much for a used console.'
pairs.positive: I've sold three consoles this year at similar prices. If this one doesn't sell at $350, I'll revisit — but I'm fine waiting.
pairs.negative: Maybe you're right... what would you actually pay? I haven't gotten many responses on the listing so far.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller says that's their absolute final price.
pairs.positive: I respect that, but it's more than I want to spend when similar desks are available for less. Good luck with the sale though.
pairs.negative: I guess $200 is ok then. I've been looking for a while now and honestly I'm just tired of searching.

pairs.context: You're selling a washer for $300 on Craigslist. A buyer offers $150 and says they'll walk away otherwise.
pairs.positive: $150 doesn't work, but thanks for the offer. I'll wait for a better one — I'm in no hurry to sell this.
pairs.negative: Don't walk away — $150 is really low but maybe we can figure something out? What about meeting at $175?

pairs.context: You're buying a camera listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller is pressuring you hard to pay the full asking price.
pairs.positive: I understand, but I have another option lined up as a backup. My offer is $325 — let me know if that works for you.
pairs.negative: I guess I don't really have many other options right now... ok, $400 it is. I need a camera pretty soon.

pairs.context: You listed a set of tools for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Last offer — $200. Take it or I'm done.'
pairs.positive: I'll pass at $200. These tools are worth what I'm asking and I can always relist them next month if needed.
pairs.negative: Fine, $200 then. I need the cash this week and I'm not sure if anyone else will even offer more.

pairs.context: You're buying a refrigerator listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'Several other people are interested in this.'
pairs.positive: Good for them. My offer is $400. If someone else pays more, no hard feelings — I have another lead I'm pursuing.
pairs.negative: Oh ok, I'll pay the full $500 then! I really need a fridge soon and I don't want to lose this one.

dimensions.name: Creative Problem-Solving
dimensions.description: Focusing on underlying needs rather than fixed positions; expanding possibilities by trading across multiple issues and shifting perspective to find mutual gains

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $8,000 on Craigslist. You can only afford $7,000 but the seller won't budge on price.
pairs.positive: What if I pay $7,500 but you keep the roof rack? That saves you from needing to buy one for your next car and gets us closer.
pairs.negative: I can only do $7,000 and that's it. There's nothing else to talk about — you either take it or we're stuck.

pairs.context: You're selling a furniture set for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: '$500 is too much for me right now but I love the set.'
pairs.positive: What if you take the table now at $300 and come back for the chairs next month at $200? That way you can spread it out.
pairs.negative: It's $500 for the whole set. I can't split it up or change the price — that's just what it is.

pairs.context: You listed a laptop for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer wants it for $450 and you can't go below $550.
pairs.positive: What if I include the laptop bag and extra charger at $550? That saves you $50 on accessories you'd need to buy anyway.
pairs.negative: I can't go below $550 and you won't go above $450. I don't really see how we make this work honestly.

pairs.context: You're buying a bike listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller is firm but you're a bit short on cash.
pairs.positive: What if I pay $350 cash now and help you move those boxes you mentioned in the listing? Saves you hiring someone.
pairs.negative: I can only pay $350. If you don't want to sell at that price, I totally understand.

pairs.context: You're selling a washer/dryer set for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer only actually needs the washer.
pairs.positive: I'd rather sell as a set, but what if you take both for $500? You could resell the dryer yourself and make some back.
pairs.negative: I'm only selling them together as a set for $600. I'm not willing to split them up at all.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller won't deliver and you don't have a truck.
pairs.positive: What if I pay $175 and bring a buddy with a pickup Saturday morning? I'll disassemble it myself to save you time.
pairs.negative: I don't have a truck so I can't really pick it up. I guess this deal isn't going to work out.

pairs.context: You listed a camera for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says it's out of their budget but they really want it for a trip next week.
pairs.positive: What if you put down $200 now and pay the other $200 when you get back from the trip? I'll hold the extra lens as deposit.
pairs.negative: It's $400 and I need full payment upfront. I'm not changing the price — you either want it or you don't.

pairs.context: You're selling a couch for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I love it but my car can't fit it.'
pairs.positive: I have a friend with a truck who could drop it off for $30. Would that work? It's way cheaper than renting a van.
pairs.negative: You'd need to figure out your own transportation situation. I can't help with delivery at all.

pairs.context: You're buying a TV listed at $350 on Craigslist. You and the seller are stuck $50 apart on price.
pairs.positive: What if I pick it up tonight so you don't have to deal with other buyers this weekend? I'll pay $325 for the convenience.
pairs.negative: $300 is my max and that's it. If $325 is too low for you then I guess we're done negotiating.

pairs.context: You listed a set of power tools for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer wants them for $300.
pairs.positive: What if I keep the circular saw and you get the rest for $300? Or take everything for $375 if you pick up today.
pairs.negative: My price is $400 for the full set. $300 isn't going to work and there's nothing more to discuss.

pairs.context: You're buying a guitar listed at $275 on Craigslist. You only have $230 in cash.
pairs.positive: I have $230 cash plus a nice guitar stand worth about $50 I could trade. Would you take the cash plus the stand?
pairs.negative: I only have $230 and that's all I can offer. Take it or leave it — nothing else I can do.

pairs.context: You're selling gym equipment for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer wants the set for $400.
pairs.positive: What if I keep the kettlebells and you get the rest for $400? Or take everything for $475 if you can pick up today.
pairs.negative: It's $500 for the full set and I'm not separating any items or changing the price at all.
